observations.recommendation: 2. Further, we recommended that the same Committee conduct physical count of PPE annually to be reported on the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for submission for audit not later than January 31 of each year.
3. Moreover, we recommended that Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards and Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment be reconciled with the annual RPCPPE.
5. Submit the List of PPAs (complete with necessary details) for audit at the beginning of every year;
6. Post the information in the List of PPAs in appropriate places using the prescribed sizes of signboards / public notices;

7. Inform the concerned audit team (within 10-day after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity) that the signboards / public notices are already posted (for validation purposes); and
8. Maintain records on the Project Status of PPAs (updated on the basis of the prescribed monthly Monitoring Report of PPAs verified by the COA TAS).

9. We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, we recommended that Management recognize a “provision” for the outcome of the reconciliation in the amount as could be reliably estimated.

10. We recommended that Management ensure that a copy of contract documents (such as memorandum of agreement, purchase order and supporting documents) be submitted for contract review within the prescribed 5-day period from the execution / issuance of the contract pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001.

11. We recommended that Management notify the COA to witness any inspection and acceptance of procurements made by Management or to validate any monthly monitoring report of Management on the status of projects pursuant to Section 6.6 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and Item 3.2 COA of Circular No. 2013-004, respectively.

17. We recommended that Municipal Accountant review the dormant accounts, namely: “other receivables” of ₱200,000.00 and “other payables” of ₱100,000.00, both under the trust fund, and effect corrections in the books, if warranted.

22. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the nature and other necessary details of the dormant account “other payables”, effect any pertinent possible corrections in the books and or disclose in the ensuing financial statements all necessary information relative thereto.

24. We recommended that Management notify the COA to witness any inspection and acceptance of procurements by Management or to validate any monthly monitoring report of Management on the status of projects pursuant to Section 6.6 of COA Circular No. 95- 006 and Item 3.2 COA of Circular No. 2013-004, respectively.

1.9 We recommended that Management:
a) Require the Inventory Committee to complete the physical count
of all types of PPE duly witnessed by a COA representative and
prepare the prescribed annual RPCPPE thereon properly
reconciled with the PPE ledger cards and other pertinent
records including property cards and AREs; and
b) Submit RPCPPE for audit.

2.8 We recommended that the Honorable Mayor cause all concerned
personnel to comply with the following:
a. Immediately submit reports and documents relating to the fund
transfer to the Office of the Auditor assigned to the implementing LGU including RCIs, RoDs duly supported with paid DVs/paid payrolls complete with supporting documents;
b. Keep a separate updated subsidiary records for each transfer
whether or not a separate bank account is maintained;
c. Record the receipt and utilizations of the fund transfer and or
effect necessary adjustments/corrections/disclosures in the books and reports; and
d. Submit to the Office of the Auditor a schedule of transfers/subsidies
actually received by the LGU including the respective bank accounts being maintained therefor, if any, so to facilitate proper audit thereto.

3.9 We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of
records with the GSIS. Further, we recommended that Management
recognize a “provision” for the outcome of the reconciliation in the
amount as could be sufficiently and reliably estimated.

4.7 We recommended that Management ensure that concerned personnel
are kept abreast of the guidelines for disbursements and monitoring
of DRRMF as prescribed under Circular 2012-002. Specifically, we
recommended that Management ensure timely rendition of monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and proper
disbursements of LDRRMF-QRF duly supported with a local
sanggunian resolution and a declaration of a state of calamity.

5.8 We recommended that Management cause to submit on time the GAD
Plan and Budget and the annual GAD Accomplishment Report
pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 and PCW-
NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01.

6.7 We recommended that Management designate a focal person tasked
to ensure proper publicity and monitoring of PPAs pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2013-004. Specifically, we recommend that
Management:
a) Submit the List of PPAs (complete with necessary details) for
audit at the beginning of every year;
b) Post the information in the List of PPAs in appropriate places
using the prescribed sizes of signboards / public notices;
c) Inform the concerned audit team (within 10-day after the award of
the infrastructure project or before the start of the
program/activity) that the signboards / public notices are already
posted (for validation purposes); and
d) Maintain records on the Project Status of PPAs (updated on the
basis of the prescribed monthly Monitoring Report of PPAs
verified by the COA TAS).

7.6 We recommended that Management require the Municipal
Accountant to facilitate timely preparation and submission of the
monthly BRSs to the Audit Team for review as required under Section
74 of PD No. 1445 and Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011.
Management may confer with the concerned depository banks to
address alleged delays in the production of bank statements.

8.7 We recommended that Management ensure that a copy of contract
documents (such as memorandum of agreement, purchase order and
supporting documents) be submitted for contract review within the
prescribed 5-day period from the execution / issuance of the contract
pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001.

9.8 We recommended that Management ensure timely submission of
monthly reports for audit such as RoD, RCI, RCD, CRAAF and BRS
together with their supporting documents.

10.7 We recommended that settlement of the suspension of disbursements
totaling ₱4,795,537.58 be made within the 90 calendar days from
receipt of the notice of suspension. Further, direct effective
disbursement controls be installed to ensure that disbursements are
duly supported with all necessary documents.
1. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee possessing competence to conduct physical count of PPE.

4. We recommended that Management ensure that concerned personnel are kept abreast of the guidelines for disbursements and monitoring of DRRMF as prescribed under Circular 2012-002. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure timely rendition of monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and proper disbursements of LDRRMF-QRF duly supported with a local sanggunian resolution and a declaration of a state of calamity.

12. We recommended that Management create a special account in the general fund (SAGF) for the BGCM account in the general fund to facilitate proper monitoring / tracking of the utilization of the funds.
13. We recommended that Management prepare the monthly BGCM Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of Program/Project/Activity pursuant to DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125.
14. We recommended that Municipal Accountant attach JEV to the paid DV/payroll and other JEV requiring source document for submission for audit.

15. We recommended that Management cause to submit on time the GAD Plan and Budget and the annual GAD Accomplishment Report pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 and PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01.

16. We recommended that Management ensure timely submission of monthly reports for audit such as Report of Disbursements (RoD), Report of Checks Issued (RCI), Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD), Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) and Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) together with their supporting documents.

18. We recommended that Municipal Accountant disclose the prior year unutilized balance of the LDRRM fund as a continuing appropriation – special trust fund of ₱696,667.36 and or effect pertinent corrections in the books and in the FS.
19. We recommended that Municipal Accountant reconcile the SCBAA and the SFP and effect appropriate corrections in the books.

20. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer review the beginning balance of the SCBAA for the year ended December 31, 2020, effect necessary corrections in the books, if any, and disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements necessary information pertinent to the beginning balance of the SCBAA.

21. We recommended that settlement of the suspension of disbursements totaling ₱3,005,517.59 be made within the 90 calendar days from receipt of the notice of suspension. Further, we recommended that effective disbursement controls be installed to ensure that disbursements are duly supported with all necessary documents.

23. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all completed trust projects be transferred to the books of accounts for General Fund, if warranted, pursuant to the existing accounting rules.

25. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the prescribed disbursement procedures are strictly followed to enable rendition of disbursement reports pursuant to Sections 9, 44, and 49, Volume 1, of the NGAS for LGUs. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure that RCI and ROD including supporting vouchers/payrolls are submitted to the COA not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month pursuant to Section 100 of PD 1445.

1. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee composing of personnel possessing competence to conduct physical count of PPE. Further, we recommended that the same Committee conduct physical count of PPE annually to be reported on the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for submission for audit not later than January 31 of each year. Moreover, we recommended that Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards and Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment be reconciled with the annual RPCPPE.

2. We recommended that Management ensure that concerned personnel are kept abreast of the guidelines for disbursements and monitoring of DRRMF as prescribed Circular 2012-002. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure timely rendition of monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and proper disbursements of LDRRMF-QRF duly supported with a local SANGGUNIAN resolution and a declaration of a state of calamity.

7. We recommended that Management fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, we recommended that Management recognize a “provision” for the outcome of the reconciliation in the amount as can be reliably estimated and appropriate fund to address eventual payment thereof.

8. We recommended that Management ensure that a copy of contract documents (such as memorandum of agreement, purchase order and supporting documents) be submitted for contract review within the prescribed 5-day period from the execution / issuance of the contract pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001.

11. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the nature and details of the dormant account “other payables”, effect any pertinent possible corrections in the books and or disclose in the ensuing financial statements all necessary information relative thereto.

13. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all completed trust projects be transferred to the books of accounts for General Fund, if warranted, pursuant to the existing accounting rules.

14. We recommended that Management notify the COA to witness any inspection and acceptance of procurements by Management or to validate any monthly monitoring report of Management on the status of projects pursuant to Section 6.6 of COA Circular No. 95-006 and Item 3.2 COA of Circular No. 2013-004, respectively.

16. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the prescribed disbursement procedures are strictly followed to enable timely rendition of disbursement reports pursuant to Sections 9, 44, and 49, Volume 1, of the NGAS for LGUs. Specifically, we recommended that Management ensure that RCI and ROD including supporting vouchers/payrolls are submitted to the COA not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month pursuant to Section 100 of PD 1445.

12.4 We recommended that Municipal Accountant review the dormant
accounts, namely: “other receivables” of ₱200,000.00 and other payables
₱100,000.00, both under the trust fund, and effect corrections in the
books, if warranted.

13.5 We recommended that Municipal Accountant disclose the prior year
unutilized balance of the LDRRM fund as a continuing appropriation –
special trust fund of ₱696,667.36 and or effect pertinent corrections in the
books and in the FS.

14.10 We recommended that Municipal Accountant reconcile the SCBAA and
the SFP and effect appropriate corrections in the books.

15.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Budget Officer review the beginning balance of the SCBAA for the year
ended December 31, 2020, effect necessary corrections in the books, if
any, and disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements necessary
information pertinent to the beginning balance of the SCBAA.

16.7 We recommended that settlement of the suspension of disbursements
totaling ₱3,005,517.59 be made within the 90 calendar days from receipt
of the notice of suspension. Further, we recommended that effective
disbursement controls be installed to ensure that disbursements are duly
supported with all necessary documents.
9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure recognition of “subsidy to other funds” as “subsidy from other funds” in the SEF books.
10. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly classify current and non-current portions of loans payable in the Statement of Financial Position.

12. We recommended that concerned personnel review the “actual amount of surplus / (deficit)” as reflected in the Statement of Comparison between Budget and Actual Amount (SCBAA) for correction of possible misstatement. Further, we recommended that the “actual amount of actual surplus / (deficit)”, not earmarked for specific purposes, be carried forward as “actual amount of beginning balance” to the ensuing SCBAA or be disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements if such amount is earmarked for specific purposes.

15. We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the limits of grant and use of cash advances as defined under COA Circular 97-002. Specifically, we recommended that concerned personnel refrain from drawing of cash advances for procurements. Management may maintain a Petty Cash Fund to defray recurring petty operating expenses for a month.

17. We recommended that Management cause for the prompt submission of the required documents to support the disbursements in the sum of ₱ 1,027,360.20. Further, we recommended that Management ensure strict adherence to the prescribed disbursement procedures as prescribed under the New Government Accounting System for LGUs.

We recommended that Management ensure that monthly Pre-Closing Trial Balance duly supported by a SAAOB, for both current and continuing appropriations, be submitted for audit not later than the twentieth (20th) day after the end of each month.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce rendition of monthly reports in particular to RCD and CRAAF. As enforcement measures, Management might consider employing or designating additional personnel for the proper discharge of collecting and disbursing functions including provision of proper training to ensure work competence.

We recommended that Management enforce due compliance with the requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004, specifically the following: 
14. At the beginning of the year, the Supervising Auditor (SA) and the Audit Team Leader (ATL) should be provided with a list of all- on-going government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year. The list shall include the project name; implementing unit, office or division if is not the agency as a whole; brief description of the PPA; contractor or suppliers, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or approved budget; project duration including start and completion dates; and location; 
15. Provide the ATL with a monthly “Project Status” based on the data of the monthly monitoring report of PPAs prepared by the Municipality; and 
16. Inform the SA and the ATL within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices.
We recommended that the Municipality should fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS.

We recommended that Management ensure proper recording of subsidies and transfers. Specifically, we recommended that the account “Subsidy from Other Funds (not Special Education Tax)” be used to recognize the receipt of the subsidy in the books for Special Education Fund.

We recommended that the LGU should ensure proper preparation of financial statements adherent to the principle of full disclosures and transparency. Specifically, significant information concerning entries appearing in particular to the cash outflows from operating and investing activities of the Statement of Cash Flows should be fully disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements or, at least, supported by schedules showing details of the entries.
We recommended that completed projects should be taken out from the books of the Trust Fund and transferred to the books of the General Fund.

We recommended that concerned personnel ensure proper recordings of the loan and the heavy equipment funded from such loan (implemented). In addition, concerned personnel must ensure that all pertinent documents (i.e. JEV, DVs, contract, bidding documents, management inspection and acceptance report, etc.) be submitted to us for post-audit, final safekeeping and for use as inputs for technical inspection of the equipment.
We recommended that Management ensure that contracts including supporting documents are submitted for COA review within the prescribed 5-day period reckoning from the perfection of the agreements.

We recommended that Management ensure that the concerned auditor would be given prior notification of inspections and acceptance of procurements including any stage of completion of any particular project.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from drawing additional cash advances prior to the settlement of previous advances. We further recommended that the LGU should consider employing or designating additional disbursing officers to avoid multiple cash advances being drawn by a lone accountable officer.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce the operational mandates of the Office of the Municipal Assessor as regard the assessment of real property for the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to come up with a realistic tax valuation of real property for use as valid input in the books and serving as basis in enforcing collections of RPT and SEF receivables.
We recommended that Management strictly enforce timely rendition of financial reports (i.e. RCI, ROD, RCD, CRAAF and BRS).
We recommended that Management strictly enforce the keeping of a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: (a) journals, (b) ledgers and (c) registries.

We recommended adherence to COA Circular No. 2012-002 including preparation of LDRRMF Plan and Budget, maintaining separate RAAOs, monthly reporting and transfer of unexpended balances to Trust Fund as appropriate.
We recommended provision of list of PPAs at the beginning of the year, monthly Project Status, and timely notice to SA/ATL after award or before start of projects.
We recommended fast tracking reconciliation with the GSIS and recognition of a provision for probable liability including appropriate budget appropriation.
We recommended proper recording of subsidies and transfers, specifically using the account 'Subsidy from Other Funds' in the SEF books.
We recommended full disclosure of significant information in the Notes to Financial Statements or supporting schedules.
We recommended transferring completed Trust Fund projects to the General Fund books.
We recommended proper recording of the loan and equipment and submission of all pertinent documents for post-audit and technical inspection.
We recommended timely submission of contract documents within the 5-day period from execution and providing notifications for inspections.
We recommended prior notification to COA for inspections and acceptance of procurements and project stages.
We recommended refraining from drawing additional advances prior to settlement and considering designating additional disbursing officers.
We recommended enforcing mandates of the Municipal Assessor and ensuring realistic tax valuations to support collection efforts.
We recommended strict enforcement of timely rendition of required financial reports (RCI, ROD, RCD, CRAAF, BRS).
We recommended enforcing the keeping of a complete set of up-to-date records (journals, ledgers, registries).

We recommended that the Municipality should strictly enforce the provisions of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124 [Volume 1] of the Manual and PPSAS 17 which mandates the following:
3. the Municipal Accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for each category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details of the PPE account in the inventory control account in the general ledger;
4. the General Services Officer (GSO) or the Municipal Treasurer, as the case maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies and PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and,
5. that, the balance per stock card / property cards should always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit and other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE); and
6. the Municipal Treasurer [Mayor] should cause for the conduct of physical count of PPE annually and the preparation of the annual Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) which has to be submitted to the concerned Auditor not later than January 31 of each year. Ideally, an inventory committee may be created to conduct the count.
We recommended that the Municipality should provide adequate insurance protection for its property, plant and equipment from the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

We recommended that form its own Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and comply with all applicable rules and regulations of Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 to ensure protection of public health and environment.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce existing guidelines in the management of GAD Fund as prescribed under PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01 and ensure that concerned personnel are properly oriented on GAD budgeting, accounting and reporting. Specifically, ensure that the Audit Team is furnished on time a copy of the: 
17. GAD Plan and Budget pursuant to Paragraph V of COA Circular No. 2014-001; as well as the 
18. annual GAD Accomplishment Report to facilitate timely rendition of an audit action.

We recommended that Management validate the “Prior Period Errors” of ₱13,869,167.55, effect necessary corrections in the books, if any, and or disclose all necessary information in the financial statements.

We recommended that persons responsible for the audit suspensions of disbursements in the sum of ₱2,072,095.60 be required to settle the same within the timeframe prescribed under COA Circular 2009-006.
24. Further, we recommended that Management revisit existing disbursement controls of the Municipality and install appropriate measures designed to ensure that no claim would be paid unless supported by valid attachments.
We recommended that the Municipality should provide adequate insurance protection for its property, plant and equipment from the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.
We recommended that form its own Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and comply with all applicable rules and regulations to ensure protection of public health and environment.
We recommended strict enforcement of GAD guidelines and timely furnishing of GAD Plan and Budget and annual GAD Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team.
We recommended validation of the Prior Period Errors, effecting necessary corrections and disclosure of necessary information in the financial statements.

We recommended that those responsible for the suspended disbursements be required to settle within the timeframe under COA Circular 2009-006 and that Management revisit and strengthen disbursement controls to ensure claims are supported by valid attachments.

We recommended that Management ensure that monthly Pre-closing
Trial Balance duly supported by a SAAOB, for both current and
continuing appropriations, be submitted for audit not later than the
twentieth (20th) day after the end of each month.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce rendition of
monthly reports in particular to RCD and CRAAF. As enforcement
measures, Management might consider employing or designating
additional personnel for the proper discharge of collecting and
disbursing functions including provision of proper training to ensure
work competence.

We recommended that the Municipality should strictly enforce the
provisions of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual
for LGUs and the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard
(PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124 of the Manual and PPSAS
17 which mandates the following:
A. The Municipal Accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for
each category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details
of the PPE account in the inventory control account in the general
ledger;
B. The General Services Officer (GSO) or the Municipal Treasurer, as
the case maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for
supplies and PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the
same and, that, the balance per stock card / property cards should
always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit and
other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment (ARE); and
C. The Municipal Treasurer should cause for the conduct of physical
count of PPE annually and the preparation of the annual Report on
the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE)
which has to be submitted to the concerned Auditor not later than
January 31 of each year. Ideally, an inventory committee may be
created to conduct the count.

We recommended that the Municipality should provide adequate
insurance protection for its property, plant and equipment from the
General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

We recommended that Management:
A. Form its own Municipal Solid Waste Management Board; and
B. Comply with all applicable rules and regulations of Ecological Solid
Waste Management Act of 2000 to ensure protection of public health
and environment.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the mandates of COA
Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, COA Circular 2014-002
dated April 15, 2014 and RA 10121 (PDRRM), specifically on accounting
and reporting of LDRRMF such as the following:
A. Proper preparation of LDRRMF Plan and strict adherence by it in
the utilization of the DRRMF, copy furnished the COA;
B. Maintaining a separate Registry for Appropriations, Allotments and
Obligations (RAAO) for LDRRMF, namely, RAAO – for QRF,
RAAO – for MOOE and RAAO – for CO;
C. Rendering a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
LDRRMF, copy furnished the COA; and
D. Ensuring that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and
LDRRMF-MOOE are transferred to the Special Trust Fund under
the account “Trust Liability – DRRM” (Code 438) in the Trust
Funds books.

We recommended that Management enforce due compliance with the
requirements prescribed under COA Circular No. 2013-004, specifically
the following:
A. At the beginning of the year, the Supervising Auditor (SA) and the
Audit Team Leader (ATL) should be provided with a list of all- on-
going government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that
are to be implemented during the year. The list shall include the
project name; implementing unit, office or division if is not the
agency as a whole; brief description of the PPA; contractor or
suppliers, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or
approved budget; project duration including start and completion
dates; and location;
B. Provide the ATL with a monthly “Project Status” based on the data
of the monthly monitoring report of PPAs prepared by the
Municipality; and
C. Inform the SA and the ATL within ten (10) days after the award of
the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity
that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices.

We recommended that Management strictly enforce existing guidelines
in the management of GAD Fund as such prescribed under PCW-
NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01 and ensure that concerned personnel are
properly oriented on GAD budgeting, accounting and reporting.
Specifically, Management must ensure proper formulation of a GAD
Plan and Budget, timely rendition of an annual GAD Accomplishment
Report as well as proper recording and keeping of GAD transactions.
Importantly, ensure that the Audit Team is furnished a copy of the GAD
Plan and Budget on time pursuant to Paragraph V of COA Circular No.
2014-001 as well as the annual GAD Accomplishment Report to facilitate
timely rendition of an audit action.

We recommended that the Municipality should fast track the
reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, the Municipality
should determine and recognize a provision for its probable liability to
the GSIS including the setting-up of pertinent equivalent appropriation
in the budget to address eventual payment thereof.

We recommended that Management ensure proper recording of
subsidies and transfers. Specifically, we recommended that all affected
accounts in the recording of Subsidy to Other Funds account in the
amount of ₱34,786.50 be corrected in the books.

We recommended that Management validate the “Prior Period Errors”
account of ₱13,869,167.55, effect necessary corrections in the books, if
any, and or disclose all necessary information in the financial statements.

We recommended that persons responsible for the audit suspensions of
disbursements in the sum of ₱2,072,095.60 be required to settle the same
within the timeframe prescribed under COA Circular 2009-006.
Further, we recommended that Management revisit existing
disbursement controls of the Municipality and install appropriate
measures designed to ensure that no claim would be paid unless
supported by valid attachments.

We recommended that the LGU should strictly enforce the provisions of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124 of the Manual and PPSAS 17 which mandates the following: A. the chief accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for each category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details of the PPE account in the inventory control account in the general ledger; B. the general services officer (GSO) or the municipal treasurer, as the case maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies and PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and, that,, the balance per stock card / property cards should always reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit and other property records like Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE); C. the local chief executive should cause for the conduct of physical count of PPE annually and the preparation of the annual Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) which has to be submitted to the concerned Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and D. infrastructure assets should be recognized in the books of accounts as PPE so that assets recorded in the registry of public infrastructures should then be brought back to the books to start in the year 2016.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, only item D of our recommendations (25% implemented) was implemented. Items A to C remained unimplemented. AAR of their programs and activities of the LGU took much attention. Though, they promised to comply with the recommendation soon as possible.
observations.reason: According to the concerned personnel, other priority projects and activities of the LGU took much of their time and attention.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records between the LGU and the GSIS. Further, the LGU should set-up a provision with corresponding issue on appropriation equal to the probable balance of GSIS actual payables after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution of the un-reconciled payables to GSIS.
observations.managementAction: The LGU is now up-to-date in making remittances of current dues to the GSIS. However, the reconciliation process as defined under the Memorandum of Undertaking (MOU) had not been completed.
observations.reason: The Municipality and the GSIS had not come up yet with the MOA for the settlement of the account. In addition, the LGU did not set up a provision nor disclose the matter in the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: A. demonstrating leadership by example; B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and C. maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a. journals, b. ledgers, c. registries; and d. cash records.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, the LGU was able to submit quite earlier, though still late, its reports, namely: monthly Reports of Checks Issued (RCIs) and Reports of Disbursements (RoDs) for audit except for RCD, CRAAF and Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS).
observations.reason: It was our impression that rendition of reports was not strictly enforced because the LGU was dependent on the Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA). Transactions involving local and other collections were quite minimal. Further, both collecting and disbursing functions were being discharged by the Municipal Treasurer alone, giving her adding difficulty to prepare reports.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our requiring top management to strictly implement necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by: A. demonstrating leadership by example; B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and C. maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a. journals, b. ledgers, c. registries; and d. cash records.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to fairly comply with our recommendations (50% implemented). Other records such as ledger cards, property cards and RAAO for LDRRMF were still deficient.
observations.reason: The books of accounts, registries and cash records were not kept updated on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LGU should ensure proper preparation of financial
statements adherent to the principle of full disclosures and transparency.
Specifically, we recommended that all necessary information concerning entries
appearing in particular to the cash outflows from operating and investing activities
of the Statement of Cash Flows should be fully disclosed in the Notes to Financial
Statements or, at least, supported by schedules showing details of the entries.

We recommended that concerned personnel should examine “the Subsidy to Other
Funds” account and effect necessary corrections in the books. For preparation of
the Statement of Financial Performance in the future, we recommended that all
necessary information relating to “subsidies, transfers and assistance” accounts
should be fully disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant ensure proper rendition of financial
statements with full disclosures of information such as prior period adjustments.
Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should identify the cause of the adjustments
and effect necessary corrections in the books, if any.

We recommended that completed projects should be taken out from the books of the
Trust Fund and transferred the same to the books of the General Fund.

We recommended Top Management strictly enforce the rendition of monthly
reports in particular to RCD and CRAAF. As enforcement measures, Management
might consider employing or designating additional personnel to supplement
existing manpower for the job including provision of proper training among
concerned employees to ensure work competence.

We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records with
the GSIS. Further, the LGU should determine and recognize its probable payables
to the GSIS including the setting-up of pertinent equivalent appropriation in the
budget to address eventual payment thereof.

We recommended that the LGU should strictly enforce the provisions of the New
Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs and the Philippine
Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS), in particular to Sections 114 and 124
of the Manual and PPSAS 17 which mandates the following:
a) the Municipal Accountant should maintain PPE Ledger Card for each
category of PPE. Such ledger cards should contain the details of the PPE
account in the inventory control account in the general ledger;
b) the General Services Officer (GSO) or the Municipal Treasurer, as the case
maybe, should maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies and
PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and, that, the
balance per stock card / property cards should always reconcile with the
ledger cards of the accounting unit and other property records like
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE); and
c) the Municipal Mayor should cause for the conduct of physical count of PPE
annually and the preparation of the annual Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) which has to be submitted to the
concerned Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned personnel to ensure
proper recordings of the loan and the heavy equipment funded from such loan. In
addition, concerned personnel must ensure that all pertinent documents (i.e. JEV,
DVs, contract, bidding documents, management inspection and acceptance report,
etc.) be submitted to us for post-audit, final safekeeping and for use as inputs for
technical inspection of the equipment.

observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, only item D of our recommendations was implemented. Items A to C remained unimplemented. The LGU took much of their time and attention. Though, they promised to comply with the CY 2016 recommendation soon as possible.
observations.reason: According to the concerned personnel, other priority projects, programs and activities of the LGU took much of their time and attention.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records with the GSIS. Further, the LGU should determine and recognize its probable payables to the GSIS including the setting-up of pertinent equivalent appropriation in the budget to address eventual payment thereof.
observations.managementAction: The LGU is now up-to-date in making remittances of current dues to the AAR as to the un-reconciled account per audit finding. Both corresponding agencies are still in the process of implementation.
observations.reason: The LGU and the GSIS had not come up yet with the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) for the settlement of the account. In addition, the LGU should determine whether its contingent liability with the GSIS had a certainty as to require recognition of a provision for contingent liability in the books.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by:
A. demonstrating audit leadership by example;
B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and
C. upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, the LGU was able to submit nearly on time its Reports of Checks Issued (RCIs) and Reports of Disbursements (RoDs) except for Reports of Collections and Deposits (RCD), Consolidated Reports of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAFs) and Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS). It was our impression that rendition of RCD, CRAAF and BRS might not have been strictly enforced because the reports seemed to have less importance to the management considering that the LGU was highly dependent on the Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA). Further, both collecting and disbursing functions were being discharged by the Municipal Treasurer alone, giving her adding difficulty to prepare reports.
observations.reason: The LGU still failed to render on time other monthly reports, namely: Reports of Collections and Deposits (RCD), Consolidated Reports of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAFs) and Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS).

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation requiring top management to strictly implement necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by:
A. demonstrating leadership by example;
B. training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and
C. maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely:
a. journals,
b. ledgers,
c. registries; and
d. cash records.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to fairly comply with our CY 2016 recommendations as items A to C. It was able to address quite remarkably our audit finding especially its books of accounts, registries and cashbooks.
observations.reason: Other records such as ledger cards, property cards and RAAO for LDRRMF were still deficient.

observations.recommendation: We recommended  that the LGU  should fast  track the  reconciliation  of records  between the LGU  and the GSIS.  Further, the LGU  should set-up a  provision with  corresponding  appropriation  equal to the  probable balance  of actual  payables after  reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution of the un-reconciled payables to GSIS..

We reiterated our recommendation requiring top management  to  strictly implement necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by: a) demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and c) maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a) journals, b) ledgers, c) registries, and d) cash records.

We recommended that management should strictly implement necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by: demonstrating leadership by example; training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: journals, ledgers, registries, and cash records.
Cause the immediate submission of the required documents within the period prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-009.

We recommended that the LGU should fast track the reconciliation of records between the LGU and the GSIS.  Further, the LGU should set-up a provision with corresponding appropriation equal to the probable balance of actual payables after reconciliation or, at least, disclose a contingent liability pending resolution of the un-reconciled payables to GSIS.

We recommended that top management should strictly enforce necessary reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability by: a) demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and c) upholding due compliance with laws, rules and regulations on the rendition of financial reports.

We reiterated our recommendation requiring top management to strictly implement necessary reforms for fiscal accountability by: a) demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to gain work competence and or hiring additional qualified personnel and or restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right person; and c) maintaining a complete set of up-to-date records, namely: a) journals, b) ledgers, c) registries, and d) cash records.

We recommended  that the  Municipal  Accountant  should make  necessary  corrections in  the recording of  PPE by  transferring to  the general fund  books all assets  representing  constructions that are fully  liquidated /  completed  pursuant to  Section 104 of  the NGAS for  LGUs.

We recommended that top management should  strictly  enforce  necessary  reforms for fiscal reporting and accountability  by:  a)  demonstrating leadership by example; b) training personnel to  gain  work  competence and or hiring  additional  qualified  personnel  and  or  restructuring existing personnel giving the right job for the right  person; and c)  upholding due compliance  with  laws,  rules  and  regulations  on  the rendition of financial reports.
We recommended that management should strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-NEDA-DBM JC 2012-01 in particular to the due rendition of GAD Plan and Budget as well as GAD Accomplishment Report

The LGU was able to come up with a
“GAD Plan
and
Budget”
observations.reason: The LGU failed to render “GAD Accomplishment Report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should strictly enforce the operational mandates of the Office of the Municipal Assessor in particular to the assessment of real property for the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to come up with a realistic tax valuation of real property for use as valid input in the books and serving as basis in enforcing collections of RPT and SEF receivables.
